questions.stem: A public health nurse is investigating a cluster of foodborne illness cases in a community following a church potluck dinner. What is the correct sequence for outbreak investigation according to epidemiologic principles?

options.text: Implement control measures → identify cases → form hypothesis → test hypothesis
Identify and count cases → establish case definition → form hypothesis about source → implement control measures → test hypothesis
Notify media → quarantine all attendees → collect stool cultures → identify the organism
Form hypothesis first → identify cases → implement controls → notify CDC

questions.rationale: Epidemiologic outbreak investigation sequence (CDC framework): (1) Prepare for fieldwork; (2) Establish existence of an outbreak; (3) Verify diagnosis; (4) Construct a working case definition; (5) Find and count cases (line listing); (6) Perform descriptive epidemiology (person, place, time — epidemic curve); (7) Develop hypotheses; (8) Evaluate hypotheses (case-control or cohort study); (9) Implement control and prevention measures; (10) Communicate findings. Cases are identified and counted before hypotheses are formed; controls are implemented based on evidence, not assumption. Notification goes to appropriate health authorities, not media first.

distractorRationales.a: Implement control measures → identify cases → form hypothesis → test hypothesis is not the best answer, and misses that (10) Communicate findings. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Identify and count cases → establish case definition → form hypothesis about source → implement control targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first. Nursing safety depends on matching the option to the client's active clinical risk.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Notify media → quarantine all attendees → collect stool cultures → identify the organism is that it waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, and misses that (10) Communicate findings. Identify and count cases → establish case definition → form hypothesis about source → implement control better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Form hypothesis first → identify cases → implement controls → notify CDC is that it waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, when the bedside priority is driven by (10) Communicate findings. Identify and count cases → establish case definition → form hypothesis about source → implement control is safer because it addresses clinical safety risk before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Outbreak investigation: identify cases first → case definition → descriptive epidemiology → hypothesis → test hypothesis → implement controls; evidence-based sequence prevents premature conclusions.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse has four patients to assess. Which patient should the nurse see FIRST, applying Maslow's hierarchy of needs?

options.text: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure
A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%
A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home
A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10

questions.rationale: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs at the foundation. Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. A patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern that takes absolute priority. Anxiety (A) is a safety/psychological need. Discharge teaching (C) is a self-actualization/cognitive need. Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure.

distractorRationales.a: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10 fits a lower-risk interpretation, although the nurse should be guided by Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88% because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Maslow hierarchy on NCLEX: airway/breathing/circulation (physiological) always come before safety, love/belonging, esteem, and self-actualization.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: The problem with Age >65 and female gender is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, although the nurse should be guided by Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. That mismatch risks slower recognition of medication toxicity and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29 is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, although the nurse should be guided by Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. The safer answer is History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic because delaying medication-safety intervention can worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: The problem with Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion is that it normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, despite the clinical warning in Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Choosing it can delay medication-safety intervention and leave medication toxicity unresolved.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: This choice focuses on Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea, despite the clinical warning in UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.b: This option makes Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube the focus, and misses that Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care, although the nurse should be guided by Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so The priority is Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.

questions.stem: A patient delivers vaginally and 30 minutes later has an estimated blood loss of 750 mL with a soft, boggy uterus displaced to the right. BP is 94/58, HR 118. Which sequence of interventions is correct?

options.text: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider
Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement
Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion
Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. (2) Empty the bladder — a full bladder displaces the uterus and prevents contraction (uterus displaced to the right = full bladder). (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. (4) Notify provider if no response. Bladder emptying is critical and often overlooked — the displaced, boggy uterus signals bladder distension. Epinephrine (D) and hysterectomy are not indicated at this stage.

distractorRationales.a: The reasoning trap in IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider is that it waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, when the bedside priority is driven by BP is 94/58, HR 118. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for bleeding-risk intervention while bleeding or anticoagulation injury progresses.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion is that it waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, but the stem cue is BP is 94/58, HR 118. It is less defensible because Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent as the key issue, while the decisive cue is (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so The safer answer is Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement because delaying bleeding-risk intervention can worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus displaced to the right after delivery = full bladder; empty bladder first, then fundal massage + oxytocin.

questions.stem: A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth, and swelling. Vital signs are stable. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses
Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result
Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound
Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: Unilateral calf pain, warmth, and swelling in a post-operative patient are classic signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can be ordered promptly. Homan's sign (B) is unreliable and has been removed from clinical guidelines. Warmth and elevation (A) delay diagnosis. Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: This answer choice treats Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses as the key issue, instead of responding to A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound, not a step that delays stabilization of circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result, and misses that The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for circulation and perfusion support while circulation or perfusion progresses.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, when the bedside priority is driven by Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate. Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound is safer because it addresses circulation or perfusion before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Post-op calf pain + swelling = suspect DVT; notify provider and anticipate duplex ultrasound — Homan's sign is unreliable.

questions.stem: A client with depression says: 'I don't see the point in trying anymore. Nothing I do matters.' Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'
'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'
'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'
'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'

questions.rationale: Option B is an open-ended exploration that invites the client to elaborate, demonstrating active listening without judgment. It also screens for suicidal ideation — 'nothing I do matters' and 'no point in trying' may signal passive suicidal thinking. Option A uses false reassurance ('so much to live for') and assumes understanding without exploring further. Option C minimizes the client's experience ('everyone feels that way'). Option D is non-therapeutic confrontation that dismisses the client's feelings.

distractorRationales.a: This option overweights 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.', although the nurse should be guided by therapeutic communication safety. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: 'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.' fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in therapeutic communication safety. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: 'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.' would not address the priority, and misses that therapeutic communication safety. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.' is safer because it addresses clinical safety risk before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication: use open-ended statements to explore meaning, especially when statements may signal suicidal ideation.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95% because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer, even though the higher-risk finding is Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95% is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, even though the higher-risk finding is work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
visualRationale.conclusion: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing vital signs in the pediatric unit. Which set of vital signs is within normal limits for a healthy 3-year-old toddler?
questions.rationale: Normal vital signs for a 3-year-old toddler: HR 80–130 bpm, RR 22–37 breaths/min, BP approximately 90–100/55–65 mmHg. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 are all within expected ranges. Choice A has a bradycardic HR and adult-level BP. Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress, and RR 40 is elevated. Choice D has significant bradycardia. Key developmental milestone: vital signs normalize toward adult ranges with age — neonates have the highest HR and RR, lowest BP.

distractorRationales.a: This choice focuses on HR 68, RR 14, BP 120/80, instead of responding to Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats HR 145, RR 40, BP 80/48 as the key issue, rather than acting on Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in HR 55, RR 18, BP 110/72 is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that RR 40 is elevated. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy at 8:00 AM. Per current ASA NPO guidelines, what are the correct restrictions for solid food and clear liquids?

options.text: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid
Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery
Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, clear juice, black coffee) allowed until 2 hours before
No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk

questions.rationale: Current ASA NPO guidelines (2017, updated 2023): Solid foods, fried/fatty meals: NPO ≥8 hours. Light meals (toast, clear liquids): NPO ≥6 hours. Non-clear liquids including milk and formula: NPO ≥6 hours. Clear liquids (water, apple juice without pulp, clear carbonated beverages, black coffee or tea without cream, sports drinks): may be consumed up to 2 hours before elective procedures. Chewing gum and hard candy: treat as clear liquid — stop 2 hours before. These evidence-based guidelines replaced the outdated 'NPO after midnight' rule. The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance, and perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction.

distractorRationales.a: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid is not the best answer, when the bedside priority is driven by perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water,, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.b: Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery is not the best answer, when the bedside priority is driven by perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, is safer because it addresses unstable glucose physiology before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before; clear liquids until 2 hrs before. 'NPO after midnight' is outdated and harmful. Clear liquids: water, clear juice (no pulp), black coffee/tea.
